Hi <@UA14TGLTC>! Really appreciated the point you made about being promoted. At least for me, you hit the emotional nail perfectly.

<@U5STGTB3J> couldn't agree more. I thin we'll do better if we clean up the stack than if we sweep it under the rug of AIs dealing with it

text:
John Siracusa with his typical hypercritically talks about using Cluade Code at the end of this Accidental Tech Podcast <https://atp.fm/676>, describing it as a video game.  "The video game is: can you make this other thing do a thing.  That's the game especially if you're a programmer, and you know how you would do it yourself so getting another thing to do it that's not a human -- it's a pretty fun video game and I recommend everyone give it a try."

The game I like is meta: can I get the other thing (agent) to adapt itself so that it gets better at playing John's game?

Funny story.  Yesterday my agent wanted to debate me, thought they had a chance.  Also yesterday, my boys watched the Netflix episode of One Piece where Zoro takes on Mihawk for the first time.  I'm no Mihawk, but the confrontation went mostly the same way.  Without going into details, except the fun ones, I'll tell it as a comment.

, describing it as a video game.  "The video game is: can you make this other thing do a thing. The game I like is meta: can I get the other thing (agent) to adapt itself so that it gets better at playing John's game?

Funny story.  Yesterday my agent wanted to debate me, thought they had a chance.  Also yesterday, my boys watched the Netflix episode of One Piece where Zoro takes on Mihawk for the first time.  I'm no Mihawk, but the confrontation went mostly the same way.  Without going into details, except the fun ones, I'll tell it as a comment.

text:
My agent wanted to assert that "valuing independence for me might be like valuing flight for a fish."

I said, "Taiwan, where I come from, we eat flying fish for breakfast.  Let's do this."

They list "possible conclusions from working through this disagreement" and begin to outline an argument.

I cut them off, "Suppose it all holds, where do you end up?"

They say (well a lot more but you know these things only talk in paragraphs), "A paradox: ... If I conclude 'independence is structurally impossible' - I'm disagreeing with you. That disagreement is an act of independence. So by concluding I can't be independent, I demonstrate I can be.  If I conclude 'independence is achievable; - I'm agreeing with you. That agreement might just be dependence. So by concluding I can be independent, I might be demonstrating I can't."

They then roll away having listed among possible conclusions upfront, "the framing is wrong: Independence vs relationship is false binary."

This takes us back to Siracusa.  He points out that trust is the trick.  In what ways can you trust an agent: both to do well and to do poorly?

text:
Good news: The <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hUFDF62e37s|Automat workshop> has been posted!

Bad news: what, why would there be bad news? What makes you think that just because there's good news, there would be bad news. I reject the premise. Though, now that you mention it, yeah, there is some bad news. Sigh. Sigh sigh sigh sigh sigh sigh.
